coredata.dc:title: Nanocomposite sponge based on sodium alginate, polyvinyl alcohol, carbon dots, and woolly hedge nettle antibacterial extract for wound healing
coredata.dc:creator: Abbasinia, Sarv
Monfared-Hajishirkiaee, Reza
Ehtesabi, Hamide
coredata.dc:description: Skin ulcers associated with significant bleeding and bacterial infections can be life-threatening and pose significant challenges in clinical settings. To solve this problem, there is an increasing need for biomaterials that have hemostatic and antibacterial properties. Such biomaterials are highly desirable as they could help to promote wound healing and prevent infections, thus improving patient therapeutic outcomes. Inspired by this content, a new type of nanocomposite sponge based on sodium alginate (SA) and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) was created with the combination of carbon dots (CDs) and woolly hedge nettle extract (HNE) as an antibacterial and hemostatic wound dressing. The prepared samples were characterized by different techniques. The properties of CDs were determined by FT-IR and XRD. SEM confirmed the creation of porous and interconnected structures. The developed nanocomposite sponge showed suitable physicochemical properties such as hydrophilicity, water vapor transmission rate, water absorption, and mechanical properties. Also, the cytotoxicity and hemolysis percentage of these sponges were within the permissible range. In addition, the antibacterial activity of the sponges increased by adding HNE. Finally, In vivo studies showed that these sponge nanocomposites can have a potential healing function for wound dressing applications.

head.ce:title: Nanocomposite sponge based on sodium alginate, polyvinyl alcohol, carbon dots, and woolly hedge nettle antibacterial extract for wound healing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Life Sciences and Biotechnology, Shahid Beheshti University, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Life Sciences and Biotechnology, Shahid Beheshti University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Life Sciences and Biotechnology, Shahid Beheshti University, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Skin ulcers associated with significant bleeding and bacterial infections can be life-threatening and pose significant challenges in clinical settings. To solve this problem, there is an increasing need for biomaterials that have hemostatic and antibacterial properties. Such biomaterials are highly desirable as they could help to promote wound healing and prevent infections, thus improving patient therapeutic outcomes. Inspired by this content, a new type of nanocomposite sponge based on sodium alginate (SA) and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) was created with the combination of carbon dots (CDs) and woolly hedge nettle extract (HNE) as an antibacterial and hemostatic wound dressing. The prepared samples were characterized by different techniques. The properties of CDs were determined by FT-IR and XRD. SEM confirmed the creation of porous and interconnected structures. The developed nanocomposite sponge showed suitable physicochemical properties such as hydrophilicity, water vapor transmission rate, water absorption, and mechanical properties. Also, the cytotoxicity and hemolysis percentage of these sponges were within the permissible range. In addition, the antibacterial activity of the sponges increased by adding HNE. Finally, In vivo studies showed that these sponge nanocomposites can have a potential healing function for wound dressing applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Wound healing is the process that the skin goes through as it repairs damage from wounds (Tao et al., 2019). When the skin fails to heal properly after an injury, it can lead to various complications, such as infection and bleeding (Ye et al., 2019). Microorganisms from the environment can quickly colonize the wound site and delay the healing process. They can also invade the body and cause serious infections when the immune system is weak (Zhou et al., 2021). Moreover, the excess fluid that accumulates in the wound can facilitate the growth and spread of microorganisms (Deng et al., 2021). In recent years, researchers have developed functional wound dressings that can help in wound healing by creating an optimal environment for skin regeneration (El-Samad et al., 2022). These dressings have certain characteristics, such as being non-toxic, biocompatible, hemostatic, and able to absorb excess fluid and prevent microbial infection. They can also promote the growth of skin cells such as keratinocytes and fibroblasts to enhance skin repair. Some examples of these dressings are hydrocolloids, hydrogels, sponges, and nanofiber mats. These dressings may also contain specific biomaterials, therapeutic agents, antimicrobial agents, or growth factors to improve wound healing and prevent infection (El-Naggar et al., 2018).

Carbohydrates are potential candidates for applications in wound care and tissue engineering (Lu et al., 2017). Alginates are biocompatible polysaccharides that are derived from brown algae and have the ability to activate macrophages and increase cytokine levels in wounds, making them suitable for biological applications (Fu et al., 2016). Alginate dressings are not suitable for wounds that are bleeding heavily, or for wounds that are dry or have minimal exudate (Ishfaq et al., 2023). This is because alginate dressings rely on the interaction between the alginate fibers and the wound fluid to form a gel that can absorb excess exudate and maintain a moist environment (Cui et al., 2022). In the same direction, alginates are often combined with other polymers to create materials that have unique properties that are not present in their individual components. For example, alginates can be mixed with other polymers to produce materials that have improved flexibility, prolonged drug release, or enhanced bioavailability of therapeutic substances. These properties make these materials suitable for developing multifunctional wound dressing (Ghosal et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2019). Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) is a synthetic, non-toxic, biocompatible, and water-soluble polymer obtained from the partial hydrolysis of polyvinyl acetate and has various biomedical applications. However, PVA has limited applications in wound dressings due to poor adhesion, low gas diffusion, and weak liquid absorption properties (Wang et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2019).

Over the past decade, carbon nanostructures such as graphene, graphene quantum dots, and carbon nanotubes have been widely used in antibacterial, wound healing, and disinfection applications due to their biocompatibility (Zheng et al., 2015). Carbon dots (CDs) are a new type of zero-dimensional nanomaterials that have shown great potential in various biomedical applications (Azad et al., 2021; Ehtesabi et al., 2021; Ehtesabi et al., 2023; Yuan et al., 2016). Moreover, CDs derivatives have high activity in wound healing due to their ability to damage bacterial membranes physically, increase oxidative stress, monitor wound pH, perform photodynamic therapy, and promote fibroblast cell proliferation. Due to these attractive properties, CDs have attracted scientists' attention for use as a novel carbon nanostructure for wound healing applications (Ehtesabi and Nasri, 2021).

Antibacterial property is essential for developing materials for use in wound dressings. Many antimicrobial dressings have been used to prevent microbial infections for many years (El-Naggar et al., 2018; Guo et al., 2022). However, antibiotic-resistant bacteria have become a common problem, leading to reduced efficacy of antibiotic-loaded dressings. Therefore, herbal extracts are increasingly being considered as a possible alternative due to their multiple effects, such as antibacterial, antioxidative, and anti-inflammatory properties, which can facilitate the skin regeneration process. Various chemical and biological components in herbal extracts can heal different kinds of wounds through different pathways and mechanisms. This makes herbal extracts a potential alternative for wound healing and care (Boateng and Catanzano, 2015). Woolly hedge nettle (Stachys byzantine) is a species of flowering plant in the mint Lamiaceae family (Gülçin et al., 2004). This plant is native to Armenia, Iran, and Turkey and is cultivated as an ornamental plant in many parts of the world. Some studies have shown that woolly hedge nettle extract (HNE) has antibacterial effects against several microorganisms, including Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus and Gram-negative bacteria such as Aeromonas hydrophila, Salmonella typhi, Bacillus cereus, Escherichia coli, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, and Campylobacter jejuni (Bahadori et al., 2020). The extract of this plant can have antibacterial, antioxidant, and pain-relieving properties, which may be related to its phenolic compounds. However, these properties may vary depending on the extraction method, plant part, and season of collection. Therefore, more research is needed to determine the potential benefits of woolly hedge nettle for human health (Berger and Berger, 2017). Several studies have reported the development of polymer sponges with hemostatic and antibacterial properties for wound healing applications (Kalirajan et al., 2021). However, most of these studies focused on the use of single or binary polymer systems and did not consider the incorporation of natural extracts or nanomaterials that could enhance the hemostatic and antibacterial performance of the polymer sponges (Biswas et al., 2021; Utzeri et al., 2022).

This work has presented a novel type of nanocomposite sponge based on sodium alginate (SA) and PVA with the combination of CDs and HNE as an antibacterial and hemostatic wound dressing. CDs were characterized using FT-IR and XRD. The surface morphology of the sponge samples was investigated using a SEM. WVTR, water absorption, porosity, mechanical properties, and contact angle were also investigated. This study also investigated blood compatibility, cytotoxicity, extract release, and antimicrobial activity of the sponges. In addition, the hemostatic and blood clotting properties of the samples were investigated.

PVA (M
w =89,000–98,000), SA (medium viscosity), and glycerol (density=1.255 g/mL at 20 °C) were obtained from Sigma Aldrich, USA. Muller Hinton Agar and Dulbecco Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) were obtained from Merck, Germany.

The roots of Pastinaca sativa were used as a green precursor for the synthesis of CDs by hydrothermal methods (Shakiba-Marani and Ehtesabi, 2023). In short, 1 g of the plant is mixed with 15 mL of deionized water in a stainless steel autoclave and then placed in an oven at a temperature of 200 °C for 8 h. After taking it out from the autoclave, when the solution reaches room temperature, the brown solution is separated. After the filtration process with filter paper, the solution is placed in a centrifuge at 10000 rpm for 20 min. Finally, the supernatant was stored at 4 °C after using a 0.22 µm filter.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction from the woolly hedge nettle plant
ce:section.ce:para: Woolly hedge nettle plant was collected from the natural habitats of East Azerbaijan province in Iran. Extraction of this plant was done by soaking method using water/ethanol (1:1 v/v). 1.5 g of the desired plant was powdered and soaked with 40 mL of aqueous/ethanolic solution at room temperature for 72 h. The suspension was filtered through filter paper and centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 20 min. Finally, the supernatant was separated after centrifugation and filtration through a 0.22 μm filter. The collected extract of capped falcons was stored at 25 °C until used.

As shown in
Figs. 1, 2g of PVA was dissolved in distilled water and stirred at 90 °C for 5 h. Another mixture containing 2 g of SA in 100 mL of deionized water was dissolved separately in a stirrer for 3 h. Next, after observing the homogenous gel solution, SA solution was added to PVA. Then glycerol (2% v/v) was slowly added to the mixture. Afterward, the solution was divided into three parts. The first part was used to make the SA/PVA sample. According to previous reports, (Shakiba-Marani and Ehtesabi, 2023), 4% v/v CD was added to the second part and stirred for 30 min to complete the synthesis of the SA/PVA/CD sample. Finally, in the third part, in addition to 4% v/v CDs, 12% v/v HNE was added to prepare the SA/PVA/CD/HNE sample. Then the samples were kept in the freezer at −18 °C for 12 h. Finally, the samples were freeze-dried in a vacuum at −60 °C for 24 h.

XRD analysis was used to investigate the formation of CDs. XRD measurements were performed using a diffractometer (XRD Bruker D8 advance) with Cu Ka radiation (λ = 1.540598 Å) at 40 kV and 30 mA. To evaluate the functional groups of CDs, it was obtained from FTIR analysis (Tensor 27, Bruker, Germany) in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1.
SEM analysis (TESCAN MIRA2) was performed to investigate the surface morphology of sponge samples. To increase the conductivity, the samples were coated with gold.

A GC-MS analysis was performed on the HNE sample using an Agilent 7890 A instrument. The sample was dissolved in n-hexane and injected into a capillary column with helium as the carrier gas. The oven temperature was programmed to increase from 50 °C to 300 °C to volatilize and separate the plant compounds. To avoid sample loss or degradation, the injection and detector temperatures were set at 220 °C and 300 °C, respectively. The GC-MS chromatogram displayed the various HNE compounds in the sample, which were matched with mass spectra libraries or standards. According to the area under the curve of each peak and comparing it with the area under the whole curve, the relative percentage of each component of the extract was determined.

The porosity of the sponges was evaluated by the alcohol displacement method (Hao et al., 2020). The sponge sample was placed in absolute ethanol the preweighted dressing was submerged in absolute ethanol until it had reached its saturation, samples were taken from the ethanol, and then eliminate excess ethanol by filter paper, and immediately observed final weight. The porosity was calculated by Eq. (1):
(1)
Porosity (%)=(W
2
-W
1
)/ρV×100
here W1 (g) and W2 (g) represent the weight of dressing before and after immersing in ethanol, respectively, V (cm3) is the dressing volume before immersion, and ρ (g/cm3) represents the density of ethanol.

The static contact angle (Ɵ) of the sponges was measured by a contact angle measurement system at room temperature. A drop was poured on the samples for 5 s and a photo of the drop was taken in the fifth second. The measurements were repeated three times.

The water vapor permeability of the samples was determined by measuring the water vapor transmission rate. For this purpose, wound dressing samples were inserted into the opening of bottles containing 10 mL of water. The initial mass of the samples was measured. Then they were kept for 1 day at 37 °C and 50% relative humidity. Finally, the samples were weighed again. The WVTR of the whole system was calculated with the following Eq. (2):
(2)
WVTR=(W
i
-W
f
)/A×100 (g m−2 d−1)
here Wi (g) is the original weight of the samples and Wf (g) is the final weight. A (cm2) represents the area of the bottle opening.

A gravimetric method was used to measure the water absorption of sponges. The dried sponges were cubed, weighed, and immersed in deionized water at 37 °C. Once the residual water has been removed, sponges are weighed at regular intervals. The absorption ratio was determined based on Eq. (3):
(3)
Water absorption (%)=(m
2
-m
1
)/m
1 ×100
here m1 and m2 denote the initial and wet weights of the sponges (g), respectively.

The tensile strength (TS), Young module, and elongation at break (E) of nanocomposite sponges were determined by using the ASTMD882–91 standard technique. To perform these tests, samples with dimensions of 3 cm ×1.5 cm were used. The mechanical properties were then immediately measured at a constant extension rate of 10 mm/min at room temperature (Rahmawati et al., 2016). The experiments were repeated three times.

The antibacterial effect of sponges loaded with HNE was tested by the disk diffusion method. Staphylococcus aureus (ATCC® number 25923, Gram-positive) and Pseudomonas aeruginosa (ATCC® number 27853, Gram-negative) were chosen as representative bacteria because they frequently cause skin infections in humans. The bacteria were grown in nutrient broth and inoculated on Müller-Hinton agar plates. Then, sponges containing different amounts of HNE (4, 8, and 12%) were placed on the agar plates in the center for 24 h. Ciprofloxacin discs were used as a control group. The antibacterial activity was determined by measuring the diameters of the zones of inhibition around each sponge (Andreu et al., 2015).

The hemolytic effect of wound samples was measured according to the previous method (Slima et al., 2019). The samples were immersed in 10 mL of 0.9% saline solution and then 0.2 mL of diluted mouse blood was added to each sample. The samples were incubated for 1 h at 37 °C. For comparison, red blood cell suspension with normal saline and DI water were considered as a negative control and positive control, respectively. After incubation, the samples were centrifuged at 1500 rpm for 10 min. The ratio of hemolysis in the samples was calculated by measuring the absorbance of hemoglobin in the supernatant at 545 nm using Eq. (4):
(4)
Hemolysis
Ratio
%
=
OD
( s )
−
OD
( − )
OD
( + )
−
OD
( − )
× 100
here OD(s) is the OD of the sponges and OD (−), and OD (+) are the negative and positive controls, respectively.

In vitro, experiments were conducted to evaluate the coagulation performance of sponges. The sponges were placed in 6 cm plates and 100 μL of rat whole blood were added to these plates. Whole blood without sample was used as the control group. Then 20 μL of CaCl2 was added to each sample and placed in an incubator at 37 °C for 20 min. Finally, 15 mL of distilled water was added to the plates to separate non-coagulated blood (Zheng et al., 2021). Subsequently, the amount of hemoglobin absorption in the samples was determined at a wavelength of 542 nm by a microplate reader (Bio-Rad 680, US). The blood clotting index (BCI) of the samples was calculated using the following Eq. (5):
(5)
BCI (%)=abs
1
/abs
0 ×100
here abs1 represents uncoagulated blood solution absorption and abs0 represents control absorption.

For checking HNE-loaded freeze-dried sponges (1 cm long × 1 cm wide × 0.3 cm high) were weighted and placed in 20 mL of PBS, at 37 °C and shaken at 100 rpm for 48 h. In order to keep the volume of the releasing system constant, 3 mL of solution was removed at specific time intervals and 3 mL of fresh PBS was added (Maver et al., 2015). Absorption measured at 420 nm was used to analyze HNE levels by UV-Visible spectrophotometer. For the calculation of the HNE release rate, an Eq. (6) was used:
(6)
Release (%)=M
T
/M
0 ×100
here M0 is the amount of HNE loaded into the sponge samples and Mt is the amount of HNE released from the sponges during time (T).

The cell viability was assessed using the MTT assay. For this assay, 100 μL of cell suspension containing 5 ×105 L929 cells/well were seeded in a 96-well plate and cultured overnight in DMEM with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS), penicillin (100 U/mL), and streptomycin (100 g/mL) at 37 °C with 5% CO2 and 90% humidity until they reached about 80% confluency. The sponges were placed in a 24-well plate with DMEM culture media overnight to obtain the extract. The extract was then added to the 96-well plate with cells. After incubating for 1, 3, and 5 days, the cells were washed with PBS twice and treated with 20 μL of MTT (5 mg/mL in PBS) for 4 h at the same condition. The MTT solution was removed and DMSO was added to dissolve the formazan crystals (A Rezaei and Ehtesabi, 2022). The absorption of the samples was measured by a microplate reader (Bio-Rad 680, US). Cell viability was measured using Eq. (7):
(7)
Cell viability (%)=(Sample absorbance)/(Control absorbance)×100

Hemostatic performance of SA/PVA, SA/PVA/CDs, and SA/PVA/CDs/HNE sponges in different bleeding models in SD rats (250–280 g seven-week-old male) after sterilization by UV light for 1 h was checked. Rats were anesthetized with 3.7% chloral hydrate and fixed on the surgical board (Monfared-Hajishirkiaee et al., 2023). Animal experiments were approved by the Ethics Committee of Shahid Beheshti University (ethical code number # IR.SBU.REC.1402.012).

The middle part of the tail of each rat was cut off (wound diameter 4.5 mm). After allowing normal blood loss (15 s) and wiping with gauze, all three sponges of specified weight were placed on the wound site without any external pressure. The control group was left without any specific treatment. Hemostasis time and blood loss were measured when the bleeding stopped.

A 2 cm incision was made in the center of the upper abdomen to fully expose the liver. The fluid surrounding the liver was completely absorbed by gas to reduce experimental interference. The wounds were then covered by all three pre-weighed sponges that had been sterilized by ultraviolet light. No treatment was used for the control group. Hemostatic time and blood loss were recorded for each group.

Using surgical scissors, a deep wound with a diameter of 1 cm was created in the upper artery of the rat's leg. All three pre-weighed sponges were quickly applied to the wound site of the mice. The other group without treatment was used as the control group. The hemostasis time and blood loss of the groups were recorded.

The purpose of this test was to examine the wound healing process of male rats (200–250 g) using different samples. The rats were injected with 4% chloral hydrate as anesthesia, their skin was cleaned with 70% ethanol, and then they were shaved. A 1 cm diameter wound was made in their back region with a biopsy punch. The rats were then grouped into three treatments: SA/PVA, SA-PVA-CD, and SA-PVA-CD-HNE. Each treatment had three rats. The rats were housed in a sterile cage at room temperature (25 °C) until the wound healed. On days 0, 7, and 14, the wound site was photographed and the healing status was evaluated. On day 14, the skin tissues with wounds were removed and fixed in 4% paraformaldehyde for 48 h. Then, the tissues were embedded in paraffin and sectioned at 5 μm. The sections were stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) and observed under an optical microscope for histological analysis.

The data were presented as a mean ± SD (n=3). Graph pad Prism statistical software version 9 was used to draw graphs and analyze data. ANOVA test was considered to analyze the significance of the data when P < 0.05.

The XRD spectrum of CDs shows an amorphous structure with a broad peak at 2Ɵ=19.54°, which is related to the amorphous nature of CDs (Fig. 2 a) (A Rezaei and Ehtesabi, 2022). In addition, as can be seen from the FTIR spectrum of CDs in Fig. 2 b, there is a strong broad peak at approximately 3361 cm−1 corresponding to the OH stretching vibration, which indicates the hydroxyl groups on the surface of CDs leading to due to the hydrophilic properties of CDs. The bands at 1710 cm−1, 1637 cm−1, 1371 cm−1, and 1023 cm−1 correspond to the vibrations of CO, C-N bonds, CC, and C-O, respectively. Therefore, FTIR results show hydroxyl, carbonyl, and amine groups on the surface of CDs and confirm the hydrophilic nature of these nanoparticles (Shakiba-Marani and Ehtesabi, 2023).

The surface morphology of the sponges was evaluated by SEM analysis. One of the most essential factors in determining the homeostatic function of the sponge is its porous structure. Macropores benefit greatly from blood and water absorption/blood-induced shape recovery, while micropores can block and enrich coagulation factors and red blood cells (Monfared-Hajishirkiaee, 2023). SEM images show the difference between SA/PVA ( Fig. 2 c1 and c2), SA/PVA/CDs (Fig. 2d1 and d2), and SA/PVA/CD/HNE (Fig. 2e1 and e2). The SA/PVA/CD nanocomposite sponge has a more porous structure and the pores have a more interconnected network. In conclusion, the addition of CDs to the SA/PVA sponge likely contributes to its porous and networked structure that facilitates fluid and blood absorption, as well as the retention of red blood cells and blood clot formation, which are associated with effective hemostasis (Rezaei and Ehtesabi, 2022). When the HNE is added to the nanocomposite sponges, a more elongated homogeneous pore structure can be observed, which has a favorable porous structure (Ma et al., 2019a,2019b).

Absorption of exudate from the skin surface can be facilitated by higher porosity in the dressing. In addition, high porosity facilitates the transport of nutrients and oxygen to the cells attached to the dressing (Meng et al., 2021). The SA/PVA sample shows a porosity of 55.6%, while SA/PVA/CD and SA/PVA/CD/HNE show a higher porosity of 73.6% and 79.3%, respectively, which is consistent with the SEM results (
Fig. 3 a). The porosity of the wound dressing can contribute to its antibacterial activity by absorbing more exudate and maintaining a moist environment in two ways (Wan et al., 2020). First, by absorbing excess exudate, the porous wound dressing can reduce the risk of maceration and infection and remove bacteria and debris from the wound surface (Alavi and Nokhodchi, 2022). Second, by retaining some moisture, the porous wound dressing can create a humid microenvironment that enhances the activity and release of the antibacterial agents and also supports the natural healing process of the wound (Zheng, Li, Luo, and Wang, 2020).

Surface hydrophilicity is an important feature in biological wound dressings (Silvestro et al., 2020). In order to observe the hydrophilic or hydrophobic behaviors of the samples, their contact angles were tested. As can be seen in Fig. 3 b, the contact angles of all sponge samples are less than 90◦, which indicates that all samples are hydrophilic. The angles of these three sponges (SA/PVA, SA/PVA/CD, and SA/PVA/CD/HNE) were determined as 77◦, 70◦, and 65◦ respectively. Alginate has a hydrophilic nature and with the addition of CDs due to the presence of hydroxyl group on the surface of this nanoparticle, hydrophilicity is improved. An increase in the hydrophilic potential of alginate sponges in the presence of plant CDs has also been reported in recent studies (Rezaei and Ehtesabi, 2022). Apparently, plant extract can improve hydrophilicity by adding hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the nanocomposite surface (Jones and Kinghorn, 2012). Therefore, SA/PVA/CD/HNE sponges can effectively absorb tissue exudate and maintain a moist environment to facilitate wound healing.

Wound dressing should be appropriate to prevent excessive drying or wetting of the wound and provide a moist environment for natural wound healing. The gas permeation pores of the wound dressing should also help protect the wound from infection and dehydration (Georgakilas et al., 2015). The WVTR of the sponge samples was measured and shown in Fig. 3 c. WVTR of sponges increased from 1954% (SA/PVA) to 2254% (SA/PVA/CD) by adding CDs. According to the results, the addition of CDs and HNE made a significant difference in the amount of water vapor transfer. It has been reported that the WVTR of nanocomposite sponges increases with the addition of plant CD due to the increase in porosity and pore size (Shakiba-Marani and Ehtesabi, 2023).

A sponge wound dressing with rich and interconnected pores can increase water absorption, which is beneficial for wound management and creating a moist microenvironment to accelerate wound healing (Monfared-Hajishirkiaee et al., 2023). Based on this, the water absorption capacity of sponge samples was investigated as shown in Fig. 3 d and e. The water absorption capacity for SA/PVA, SA/PVA/CD, and SA/PVA/CD/HNE sponges was estimated to be 546%, 741%, and 813%, respectively. The addition of CDs increased the water absorption of wound dressings, which may be due to the presence of abundant hydrogen and ionic bonds in the highly hydrophilic CDs structure (Shakiba-Marani and Ehtesabi, 2023). These results were consistent with the contact angle measurement results.

An ideal wound dressing should have moderate mechanical strength and flexibility. This is important for resistance to arterial hypertension and fracture prevention (Singh and Dhiman, 2016). Tensile strength tests were performed to evaluate these properties. The mechanical properties of sponges were significantly affected by the presence of CDs. By adding CDs and extract content, the tensile strength of SA/PVA, SA/PVA/CD, and SA/PVA/CD/HNE sponges was 0.64, 0.82, and 0.84 MPa respectively ( Fig. 4 a). In addition, the inclusion of CD and HNE increased Young's modulus of the samples from 0.23 to 0.39 MPa (Fig. 4 b). CDs and HNE can lead to an increase in the density of crosslinks of SA and PVA, as a result of which molecular movements are limited. Probably because of this, the elongation at break decreased from 44% to 27% (Fig. 4 c and d). However, the sponges retained their integrity, which is important for wound dressings. The increase in tensile strength and Young's modulus of sponge wound dressing composed of SA, PVA, and CD compared to the sponge without CDs, the formation of hydrogen bonds, and crosslinks between CDs and polymer matrix have been reported in recent studies (Yu, Zhang, and Guo, 2022). These findings show that the sponges prepared in this study have good potential with the necessary strength for wound healing (Chen et al., 2018).

The main obstacle to wound healing is bacterial infection, which can increase the production of exudate and delay the healing process (Rezaei, Ehtesabi, and Ebrahimi, 2023). Therefore, one of the desired features of a successful wound healer is the ability to fight against bacteria and other microorganisms on the skin's surface (Alsakhawy, Baghdadi, El-Shenawy, Sabra, and El-Hosseiny, 2022). In this context, using natural antimicrobial agents to treat wounds has been a major medical advancement of the recent century (Rahmoun et al., 2013). Sponge dressings were evaluated for antibacterial activity against wound-related microorganisms such as Staphylococcus aureus and Pseudomonas aeruginosa strains. Three concentrations of extract in sponges (SA/PVA/CD/HNE1 with 4%, SA/PVA/CD/HNE2 with 8%, and SA/PVA/CD/HNE3 with 12%) were used to select the optimal concentration of the extract for antibacterial activity. The order of the antibacterial potential of sponges was estimated using the residence zone test. According to the results of Fig. 5, in the Staphylococcus aureus strain, the lowest inhibition rate was related to the SA/PVA sample. The best antibacterial performance against this bacterium belongs to SA/PVA/CD/HNE3 with an inhibition diameter of 16 mm, which has the highest concentration of HNE. This concentration does not show a significant difference with the SA/PVA/CD/HNE2 sample with a diameter of 15 mm. The same trend was also true for the Pseudomonas aeruginosa strain, with the difference that this Gram-negative bacterium was generally more sensitive to the samples and the diameter of the halos of non-growth was greater than that of the Gram-positive strain. SA/PVA/CD/HNE3 sample with 20 mm inhibition showed the highest level of inhibition and SA/PVA sample with 5 mm showed the lowest inhibition level. In this case, the effect of CDs in improving the antibacterial performance was minor and compared to the sample without CDs, no significant difference was observed. The antibacterial activity of HNE is due to the presence of bioactive compounds such as germacrene D, β-pinene, limonene, β-cubenene, linalool, and β-caryophyllene which have antibacterial properties and essential for preventing wound infection (Elez Garofulić et al., 2021; Marume et al., 2017). Overall, SA/PVA/CD/HNE sponges showed high potential as wound dressings with an effective protective effect against the colonization of wound-related bacteria.

In vitro blood compatibility is a test to measure the compatibility behavior of red blood cells (RBC) in contact with foreign substances. In biomedical fields, hemocompatibility and an appropriate range of blood hemolysis must be ensured for wound dressing materials that are directly related to blood (Jiang, Li, Richard, Scherman, and Liu, 2019). According to ASTM F 756–00, biomaterials can be classified into three types based on blood compatibility. Materials with a degree of hemolysis greater than 5% are classified as hemolytic, while materials with a degree of hemolysis between 5% and 2% are considered mildly hemolytic. Materials with less than 2% degree of hemolysis are classified as non-hemolytic (A Rezaei and Ehtesabi, 2022). The blood hemolysis values of SA/PVA, SA/PVA/CD, and SA/PVA/CD/HNE were 2.19, 4.31, and 4.51%, respectively. According to the results of Fig. 6 a, the addition of CDs and HNE has a partial effect on the amount of hemolysis, but the percentage of hemolysis of all sponges is below 5% and non-hemolytic. Fig. 6 b shows the macroscopic images of the supernatant obtained after centrifuging the three sponge groups, the negative control group, and the positive control group. All three groups of sponges showed a whitish appearance, which was similar to the negative control group. However, the positive control group showed a bright red color, indicating complete hemolysis of the blood. The non-hemolytic performance of nanocomposite sponges based on CDs has been confirmed in previous reports (Rezaei and Ehtesabi, 2022).

Blood clotting index (BCI) is an important criterion for the evaluation of thrombus formation and the evaluation of blood thrombogenesis caused by hemostatic substances, and it is checked by the absorption of residual hemoglobin that does not participate in blood coagulation (Ma et al., 2019a,2019b). A low BCI means that the material has a strong hemostatic ability. To compare the coagulation efficiency of all sponges, a whole blood clotting test was performed as an initial and baseline assessment of hemostatic function. The degree of blood clotting can be determined in the initial judgment by observing the distribution of blood and the color of the water. As shown in the control group, the water turned red due to the spread of non-coagulated blood (Fig. 6 c). This means that in a short period of time, the blood hardly coagulates in its normal state. With the addition of CDs, the BCI decreased from 28.6% to 20.1%. In the presence of CDs, the whole blood spread slightly and the color of the water changed slightly, which means that the SA/PVA/CD sponge has such a strong blood clotting ability that almost no hemoglobin is released from the sponge into the water. As shown above, with the addition of CDs, the sponge had higher porosity and water absorption, thus giving it a greater ability to collect and absorb erythrocytes/platelets compared to the SA/PVA sponge (Shakiba-Marani and Ehtesabi, 2023). Furthermore, the addition of HNE had a mild positive effect on the blood coagulation process and reduced the BCI to 18.5% (Fig. 6 d). The increase of blood clotting activity by sponges composed of alginate and CDs has been confirmed in previous reports.

Increasing the success rate of wound healing, antibacterial extract may play an important role in wound healing. To prevent bacterial growth in the wound bed, HNE was used as an antibacterial agent in this report. The result of the extract release is presented in Fig. 6 e. To investigate the effect of CD in sponges on the sustained release of HNE, we investigated the cumulative release of HNE-loaded sponges in SA/PVA/CD/HNE and SA/PVA/HNE over time. The initial burst release of the drug at the wound site can be useful for killing the initial bacteria, and the subsequent slow release of the drug prevents infection, which is one of the essential features of drugs used for wound healing (Hashemikia et al., 2021). These early events begin within the first seconds after injury and last approximately 3 h. followed by rapid recruitment of neutrophils into the injured tissue within the first 24 h and a subsequent decline in the following week (Ilhan et al., 2020). Therefore, nanocomposite scaffolds loaded with HNE can be effective in the inflammatory phase in the form of controlled release for 24 h. By comparing the release patterns of SA/PVA/HNE and SA/PVA/CD/HNE sponges after the initial burst release, it was found that the addition of CDs to the sponge structure may slightly increase the drug release rate. This is probably because CDs increase sponge water absorption and improve electrostatic repulsion at pH=7.4 due to the ionization of carboxyl groups (Chizari et al., 2022). This leads to the expansion of the network structure and promotes drug diffusion. As a result, both sponges prepared to hold the drugs were able to maintain a slow release rate and eliminate the need for frequent dressing changes for up to 48 h. The results suggest that the sponges prepared with CD and HNE can be effective antibacterial and wound healing agents, as they can deliver the drug in a controlled manner to the wound site and prevent infection. The release patterns of the sponges are consistent with the previous studies on nanocomposite dressings, which have shown promising wound healing performance in vitro and in vivo (Zengin et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2020).

Biocompatible wound dressings act as synthetic matrices to support tissue regeneration and promote normal cellular metabolic pathways (Gruppuso et al., 2021). To evaluate the effect of the interaction between cells and biomaterials, oxidation activity was measured using the MTT direct contact method, which is a reliable metabolic marker for evaluating cell proliferation. As shown in Fig. 6 f, the cytotoxicity of the samples was evaluated after 24, 48, and 72 h. HNE-loaded sponges showed cell compatibility and non-toxicity against L929 cells isolated from connective tissue. The viability percentage of the samples showed that CDs and HNE can cause a slight decrease in the cell viability of the L929 cell line, although this effect cannot be considered cytotoxic. Therefore, according to the results, sponges loaded with CDs and HNE, when used on the wound, do not lead to cytotoxicity and can help the growth of skin fibroblast cells. The low cytotoxicity and high biocompatibility with skin fibroblast cells of plant extracts have been confirmed in previous reports (Ghuman et al., 2016).

To assess the efficacy of the sponges in promoting blood clotting in different types of wounds commonly found in clinical settings, various animal models were used. The results presented in Fig. 7 a show that without any treatment, cutting the tail in mice led to continuous bleeding with an average duration of 552 s and blood loss of 353 mg. However, when sponges containing CDs and HNE were used, both clotting time and blood loss were significantly reduced. Hemostatic time and blood loss were reduced to 86 s and 118 mg for SA/PVA/CD/HNE, respectively. These findings show that sponges have been effective in reducing bleeding and increasing blood clotting in this mouse tail amputation model. The liver contains the most blood vessels among all internal organs and is easily damaged when under pressure. According to the results of Fig. 7 b, the best hemostatic performance among treatment groups belonged to SA/PVA/CD/HNE with a hemostatic time of 119 s and a blood loss rate of 88 mg. In addition, as shown in Fig. 7 c, in the leg artery model in the presence of CDs and HNE, the hemostatic time of SA/PVA/CD/HNE (121 s) was the lowest, but there was no significant difference with the SA/PVA/CD sample (131 s). Also, the amount of blood loss for SA/PVA/CD/HNE was estimated to be 185 mg, which showed a slight decrease compared to the SA/PVA/CD sample. According to the results, the addition of CDs and HNE to the SA/PVA sponge resulted in increased effectiveness in reducing hemostatic time, blood loss, and promoting blood coagulation compared to the untreated group. In the presence of CDs and HNE, the absorption and calling of red blood cells, platelets, and other coagulation factors in the blood increased (Monfared-Hajishirkiaee et al., 2023). It has been reported that CDs can stimulate the extrinsic coagulation system and activate the fibrinogen system, leading to significantly shorter bleeding times than normal saline-treated rats in tail and liver bleeding experiments (Luo et al., 2018). Therefore, achieving efficient homeostasis for these nanocomposite wound dressings can make them ideal for use as a homeostatic framework with high homeostatic capacity (Lavrador et al., 2021).

The wound healing process of rats was evaluated by measuring the percentage of wound closure after 14 days of applying different sponge wound dressings. The wound healing process is influenced by various factors, such as the type and composition of the dressing, the size and depth of the wound, the presence of infection, and the host's immune system. The results showed that SA/PVA had the lowest percentage of wound closure (65%) after 14 days, indicating that it was not effective in promoting wound healing ( Fig. 8 a and b). This could be due to the low water retention capacity of PVA, which may cause dehydration and inflammation in the wound site. Moreover, PVA may not adhere well to the wound tissue, which may reduce its contact time and delivery efficiency (Niranjan et al., 2019). The results also showed that SA/PVA/CD had a higher percentage of wound closure (75%) than SA/PVA after 14 days. This suggests that CDs enhanced the performance of PVA in terms of water retention, adhesion, and antibacterial activity. CDs may also stimulate cell proliferation, migration, and differentiation in the wound tissue, which are essential for tissue regeneration (Gujju et al., 2023). The results further showed that SA/PVA/CD/HNE had the highest percentage of wound closure (97%) after 14 days. This indicates that HNE improved the efficacy of CDs in terms of antibacterial activity against various pathogens that may cause infection in the wound site. HNE may also have synergistic effects with CDs by increasing their cellular uptake and enhancing their fluorescence emission. In addition to measuring the percentage of wound closure, also performed histopathological analysis to evaluate the quality and structure of the regenerated tissue. The histopathological results showed that SA/PVA had a poor healing outcome compared to SA/PVA/CD or SA/PVA/CD/HNE. The site of the wound mainly remained denuded and there was no attempt for re-epithelialization in the lesions of this group in the margins. The wound bed was filled with relatively immature granulation tissue composed of active fibroblasts, new matrix deposition with moderately diffuse infiltration of inflammatory cells, and congested blood vessels (Fig. 8 c). On the other hand, SA/PVA/CD had a better healing outcome than SA/PVA or SA/PVA/CD/HNE. The dermal matrix was relatively covered with stratified squamous epithelium, however, its thickness was somewhat lower than in intact borders. Collagen bundles were arranged nearly parallel to the epidermis in the regenerated dermis (Fig. 8 d). Finally, SA/PVA/CD/HNE had an optimal healing outcome among all groups. The dermal matrix was fully covered with stratified squamous epithelium with normal thickness. The wound bed was mainly filled with fibroblast proliferation with well-organized collagen bundles arranged parallel to each other (Fig. 8 e). In total, the wound healing and histopathological evaluation results showed the appropriate performance for the therapeutic aspect of SA/PVA/CD/HNE sponges for use in wound dressing applications.

In this study, a nanocomposite sponge that can be used as a wound dressing for skin wounds with significant bleeding and bacterial infections was developed. The nanocomposite sponge is composed of SA/PVA as a matrix, CDs as natural nanoparticles, and HNE as a natural additive. Synthesis and characterization of nanocomposite CDs and sponges were performed by various techniques such as FT-IR, XRD, and SEM. The nanocomposite sponge showed good hydrophilicity, water vapor transmission rate, water absorption, and mechanical properties, which are important for wound dressing applications. In addition, the nanocomposite sponge has low cytotoxicity and hemolysis percentage, which indicates its biocompatibility with human cells and blood. In addition, the nanocomposite sponge showed greater antibacterial activity against common wound pathogens, such as Staphylococcus aureus and Pseudomonas aeruginosa, due to the presence of nettle extract. Finally, the nanocomposite sponge showed effective healing performance for the regeneration of damaged tissue in animal models. Therefore, the nanocomposite sponge developed in this study can be a potential antibacterial and hemostatic wound dressing for skin wounds. However, more studies are needed to optimize the nanocomposite sponge formulation and evaluate its long-term effects on wound healing in vivo.

Hamide Ehtesabi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Sarv Abbasinia: Investigation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Reza Monfared-Hajishirkiaee: Investigation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.
